summary: Gruber follows up on Apple's decision to send a push notification ad for F1 The Movie through the Wallet app, arguing it fundamentally undermines Apple's pitch for digital wallet trust. He uses a comedic Mastodon post as a springboard to make the serious point that Apple Wallet competes against physical wallets, which by nature cannot contain ads. Since Apple asks users to trust Wallet with finances, identity cards, and keys, any advertising in that space is uniquely destructive. He argues the ad damages not just actual privacy but the perception of privacy, and calls for whoever authorized it to be fired.
key_points: Apple Wallet competes against physical wallets, which inherently cannot contain ads — that's the trust bar Apple must meet
Wallet handles finances, identity cards, and keys, making it a space that should be as sacrosanct as the Passwords app
Even if the ad was indiscriminate, users who fit the movie's demographic will reasonably suspect Wallet is tracking their interests and activities
The perception of privacy is as important as technical privacy — this ad undermines both the reality and the belief that Apple can be trusted differently than other big tech companies
money_quotes: It's a fact that no company can inject an ad into your physical wallet. It just can't happen.
They're competing against the privacy and intimacy of one of the most personal things people carry with them.
Apple Wallet ought to be sacrosanct — like the Passwords and Journal apps.
Apple is asking us to trust this app with our finances, our identity cards, and our keys.
It'd be completely sensible to be spooked by that, and conclude that Apple Wallet is tracking you.
Sending this ad is completely destructive to all the hard work other teams at Apple have done to make Apple Wallet actually private.
The perception of privacy is just as important as the technical details that make something actually private.
I think whoever authorized this movie ad through Wallet push notifications ought to be canned.
key_insight: By sending an ad through Wallet, Apple violated the implicit contract that its most sensitive apps would remain ad-free, undermining the trust advantage that distinguishes it from other tech companies.
